critic_raw:
The most significant flaw is the use of a stratified random split on multi-year IoT sensor time-series. Sensor data is highly temporally autocorrelated — vibration signatures, temperature drift, and failure precursors unfold over continuous time windows. A random split will routinely place observations from the same equipment run or near-failure window in both training and test sets. The model thus sees the future (post-anomaly stabilization patterns, post-maintenance return-to-baseline) during training and exploits those signals to "predict" failures it has already partially observed. For predictive maintenance, temporal leakage is not a distributional technicality — it directly inflates anomaly detection performance because failure signatures are not i.i.d. events; they are correlated sequences.

The baseline comparison is structurally underpowered and asymmetric. A threshold-based system tuned on historical data is not a strong baseline for anomaly detection in manufacturing. The design gives no detail on how thresholds are tuned — univariate per-sensor thresholds optimized on training data are trivially beatable by any multivariate model because the threshold system cannot exploit cross-sensor covariance. The fair comparison would be a threshold system applied to learned multivariate summary statistics, or at minimum a classical multivariate control chart (Hotelling T², CUSUM). As designed, beating the baseline tells us nothing about whether CNNs specifically add value.

The primary metric — reduction in false positive rate — is evaluated in isolation without specifying what happens to false negatives (missed failures). Optimizing false positives alone is easily gamed: a model that raises no alarms has zero false positives. The design does not fix a recall threshold before comparing models, which means the reported FPR comparison may be at incomparable operating points on the precision-recall curve.

The anomaly labeling definition is underspecified. Labels are "defined based on significant deviations from normal operational patterns" — this could mean domain experts marked failure precursors, or it could mean deviations from a statistical baseline were automatically labeled. If anomaly labels were derived from the same sensor data used as model inputs, there is circular label construction: the model is trained to detect deviations that were themselves defined by deviations in the training signal.

CNN architecture for time-series anomaly detection is not a guaranteed win. The claim that CNNs offer an advantage due to "spatial hierarchies" is confused — CNNs exploit spatial translation invariance, which applies to images. For 1D time-series, the relevant inductive bias is temporal translation invariance, which vanilla CNNs provide but is also provided by LSTM, TCN, and classical spectral methods. The architecture choice is not derived from the problem structure; it is assumed.

Hourly aggregation of sensor data may destroy the failure precursor signal. Many equipment failure signatures manifest in high-frequency vibration data (sub-second resolution). Collapsing to hourly averages may eliminate exactly the features that distinguish incipient failures from normal operation, making the CNN task either impossible (if the signal is gone) or trivially easy (if hourly mean is already informative, then the threshold system should also work well on it).
defender_raw:
The CNN-based anomaly detection design is well-motivated and methodologically coherent for this manufacturing context.

The choice of a CNN for time-series sensor data is defensible. CNNs have demonstrated strong performance on time-series anomaly detection because they learn local temporal patterns through convolutional filters applied over sliding windows. The 'spatial hierarchy' framing is imprecise but the underlying capability — multi-scale feature extraction over sensor readings — is legitimate. Vibration and temperature signals exhibit periodic structure and transient signatures that convolutional filters can capture effectively.

The primary metric choice — false positive rate reduction — is the right stakeholder alignment. In manufacturing, unnecessary maintenance interventions have direct cost consequences (downtime scheduling, labor). Optimizing for FPR is appropriate when the domain priority is specificity over sensitivity. Secondary metrics (lead time, precision) complement this well by characterizing detection quality beyond just the rate of false alarms.

The preprocessing discipline is sound. Fitting normalization only on training data and applying it to validation and test is correctly specified. This prevents the subtle but common mistake of using global statistics that incorporate future data.

The threshold-based baseline is the correct comparison target for this problem. Expert-defined thresholds represent the incumbent system; comparing against a carefully tuned threshold baseline is a fair test of whether CNN modeling adds genuine lift.

The stratified random split's weakness in a temporal problem is real but not necessarily fatal. The design justifies it by treating anomaly representation across splits as the critical validity concern. For a manufacturing sensor system with multiple equipment units producing many independent time windows, some cross-sectional validity to the stratification claim exists. Whether temporal autocorrelation dominates is an empirical question the current design cannot answer definitively.

The confound controls for seasonal operational variation are mentioned but underspecified. Noting that controls exist without stating the mechanism (covariate inclusion, period exclusion, stratified analysis) leaves this aspect open.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on temporally autocorrelated sensor data causes leakage: observations from the same equipment run or failure window appear in both training and test sets, inflating anomaly detection performance artificially.

The threshold-based baseline is not tuned to exploit cross-sensor correlations, making it trivially beatable by any multivariate model; a fair baseline would be a multivariate control chart (e.g., Hotelling T², CUSUM) or a threshold over learned multivariate summaries.

False positive rate is reported as the primary metric without fixing a corresponding recall/sensitivity level; the comparison may be across incomparable operating points on the precision-recall curve.

Anomaly label construction is circular if labels are derived from statistical deviations in the same sensor signal used as model input, embedding the labeling function's assumptions into both training and evaluation.
Hourly aggregation may destroy high-frequency failure precursor signals, making the CNN either unable to learn the relevant pattern or rendering the task trivial for the threshold baseline as well.

The justification for CNN over other sequence architectures (LSTM, TCN, spectral methods) is confused — CNNs exploit spatial not temporal translation invariance, and no ablation distinguishes architecture benefit from representational capacity.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on temporally autocorrelated sensor data causes leakage: observations from the same equipment run or failure window appear in both training and test sets, inflating anomaly detection performance artificially.

The threshold-based baseline is not tuned to exploit cross-sensor correlations, making it trivially beatable by any multivariate model; a fair baseline would be a multivariate control chart (e.g., Hotelling T², CUSUM) or a threshold over learned multivariate summaries.

False positive rate is reported as the primary metric without fixing a corresponding recall/sensitivity level; the comparison may be across incomparable operating points on the precision-recall curve.

Anomaly label construction is circular if labels are derived from statistical deviations in the same sensor signal used as model input, embedding the labeling function's assumptions into both training and evaluation.
